metadata.title: The Engineer Who Stayed
metadata.segment: Why Most AI Projects Fail in Production — And One Engineer Who Bridges That Gap
metadata.audience: Humanitarians AI Fellows — engineers and researchers learning about production ML
metadata.greeting_note: Annyeong = Korean for Hello. Liam two-word budget.
metadata.source_title: Why Most AI Projects Fail in Production — And One Engineer Who Bridges That Gap
metadata.note: STUDENT/FELLOW PROFILE mode. Subject: Kaustubha Venkata Eluri. One idea: trustworthiness is a system property, not a model property. Liam narrates ABOUT Kaustubha, never AS him. @HumanitariansAI folderLabel. No statistics appear on screen — the article states none.

beats.narration_text: Profile, Kaustubha Venkata Eluri. Most AI projects don't fail because the model is wrong. They fail in the gap between 'works in the lab' and 'works when someone depends on it.' Some engineers hit that gap and hand it off. He stayed. This is Liam, in for Bear.

props.command: How does an engineer stay in the gap between 'works in the lab' and 'works when someone depends on it'?
output: The engineering that gets you used

beats.narration_text: Picture two platforms. On the left: development. Green metrics, curated data, a model that hits its numbers. On the right: production. Noisy inputs. Latency. APIs that go down. Users who don't behave the way the test set did. Most projects fall into the space between. Kaustubha came from architectural engineering — a field where the building has to stand up after you leave. He carried that into software. You're responsible for whether it keeps working.

props.sparkLine: If you build it, you're responsible.

beats.narration_text: Here's the industry blind spot. A model that works in isolation is a curiosity. The accuracy is real. The demo is impressive. But the model lives inside a system — and the system is what someone actually depends on. Integration, deployment, failure handling, reliability under load. These aren't model problems. They're system problems. Trustworthiness is a system property, not a model property. That's the thesis. Everything Kaustubha builds is evidence for it.

Four projects, all different domains, one discipline. RapidTriage AI: clinical decision support where latency and accuracy both have to hold under time pressure. Echolin dot AI: deepfake detection that needs to be robust and explainable — two requirements most systems treat as optional. Semantic Diff Prompting: making vision-language models more efficient, because efficiency is reliability. NEUQuest: a mobile platform where usability and reliability matter as much as features. Same boundary, different domains.

Here's what production actually demands. An API fails — not sometimes, regularly. One branch: the system collapses. Red from end to end. The other branch: the system degrades gracefully. One node goes amber. The rest keeps working. That's the question that shifts when you're building for real use. Not 'does this work?' but 'does this keep working when I'm not in the room?' A system that works for one user and breaks at a hundred is a demonstration with a critical flaw.

The industry celebrates the benchmark improvement and the novel architecture. The accepted paper. The citation count. What it doesn't celebrate: stable under load, graceful API handling, explainability that clinicians actually trust, latency that meets real deadlines. This is not the engineering that gets you cited. This is the engineering that gets you used. That's the uncelebrated space where this work lives.

props.sparkLine: The engineering that gets you used.

beats.narration_text: Kaustubha Venkata Eluri is at Northeastern University — Teaching Assistant in Mobile App Development, Academic Peer Mentor, Student Ambassador. You can find him at GitHub github.com slash Kaustubha-09, and LinkedIn linkedin.com slash in slash kaustubha-ve. If you're building production ML systems and need someone who makes systems reliable — not just accurate — the article is in the description. Written by Aditi Shinde for NortheasternISE, March 31, 2026.

Let's recap with Claude. Trustworthiness is not a model property — it's a system property. A model that works in isolation is a curiosity. A system that works in production is a tool. You build it by designing for failure, not against it. Degradation is not defeat. Degradation is the design.

artifactLines: A model that works in isolation is a curiosity.
A system that works in production is a tool.
Design for failure, not against it.
Degradation is not defeat. Degradation is the design.

beats.narration_text: Here's a prompt to run on your own project. Audit my ML project for the gap between works in the lab and works when someone depends on it. List what breaks under real load, noisy real-world inputs, and API failures — and for each, how to make it degrade gracefully instead of catastrophically. Run this on anything you're building. The gaps it finds are the ones that will surface in production — better to find them now, in a conversation, than in a system that's already deployed.

props.command: Audit my ML project for the gap between 'works in the lab' and 'works when someone depends on it.' List what breaks under real load, noisy real-world inputs, and API failures — and for each, how to make it degrade gracefully instead of catastrophically.

beats.narration_text: The Engineer Who Stayed. Liam, in for Bear.
